Problem Statement: In the following exercises, consider the graph of the function $$y = f ( x )$$ shown here. Which of the statements about $$y = f ( x )$$ are true and which are false? Explain why a statement is false.
Question: $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = 5$$
Answer: False; $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right)$$ DNE since $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6^{-}}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = 2$$ and $$\underset{x \rightarrow 6^{+}}{\text{lim}} f \left(\right. x \left.\right) = 5 .$$
o3 Answer:
False.  

Look at what the graph is doing when x gets close to 6.

• For x-values a little smaller than 6 the curve is on the “wiggly’’ branch whose y–values are a little above 2.  
• For x-values a little larger than 6 the curve is on the same branch and the y–values again are a little above 2.  
• At x = 6 the point on that branch is an open circle (a hole) whose y–coordinate is about 2 (≈ 2.3), so the function itself is not defined there, but the y-values of the graph on both sides are clearly converging to that same number ≈ 2.

Therefore  
lim_{x→6} f(x) ≈ 2, not 5.  

(The filled-in point near (8, 5) is irrelevant to the behaviour as x approaches 6, so claiming the limit is 5 is incorrect.)

    $$\underset{x \rightarrow x_{S F}}{\text{lim}} \rho \left(\right. x \left.\right) \quad \text{does not exist.}$$

---

### Physical Meanings

The location $$x_{\text{SF}}$$ represents the "shock front," which is the boundary separating two different states of a medium.

*   **Physical meaning of $$\underset{x \rightarrow x_{S F} ^{+}}{\text{lim}} \rho \left(\right. x \left.\right) = \rho_{2}$$**: This represents the density of the medium **just ahead of the shock front**. This is the undisturbed, ambient density of the material *before* the shock wave has passed through it.

*   **Physical meaning of $$\underset{x \rightarrow x_{S F} ^{-}}{\text{lim}} \rho \left(\right. x \left.\right) = \rho_{1}$$**: This represents the density of the medium **just behind the shock front**. This is the compressed density of the material *immediately after* the shock wave has passed through it. From the graph, we see ρ₁ > ρ₂, which is physically expected as a shock wave compresses the medium it travels through.

*   **Physical meaning of the limit not existing**: The fact that the two-sided limit does not exist is the mathematical representation of the physical concept of a **shock wave**. It signifies that there is a **discontinuity** or an abrupt, instantaneous jump in the physical properties (in this case, density) across the shock front. There is no gradual transition from the uncompressed state to the compressed state; the change happens across an infinitesimally thin boundary. This sharp discontinuity is the defining characteristic of an ideal shock wave.
